item.title: Mona Lisa
item.lesson: She is probably Lisa Gherardini, wife of the Florentine silk merchant Francesco del Giocondo — hence 'La Gioconda' — but Leonardo never delivered the portrait. He carried it with him for the rest of his life, reworking it in whisper-thin oil glazes, and died with it in France, which is how it entered the French royal collection. The famous smile is engineered: Leonardo's sfumato technique blurs the corners of the mouth and eyes, exactly the regions your peripheral vision reads, so her expression genuinely changes as your gaze moves. For centuries she was admired but not supreme; the theft of the painting in 1911 by an Italian handyman named Vincenzo Peruggia, and the two-year worldwide hunt that followed, transformed her into the most famous object humans have made. The crowds today photograph a celebrity; stay a moment longer and you can still find the painting.
item.lookFor: Notice the two horizons: the landscape behind her left shoulder sits higher than the one behind her right, a quiet impossibility that keeps the picture subtly alive.
